summary: I was about five or six years old during the Sino-Japanese War. Although I was very young, I remember hearing stories from others and even recall seeing a soldier's cap with bullet holes. It was a significant event that left an impression on me as a child growing up in Kochi Prefecture.

The Russo-Japanese War happened about ten years after the Sino-Japanese War, so I was more aware of what was going on at that time. However, I don't remember anything particularly notable from that period. It was just part of the background of my early life in Japan before I left for the United States in 1906.

World War I started in 1914, but I did not participate in the war. Since I was living in the United States during that time, I have little to say about it personally. It did not directly affect my life as far as I recall.

I know about the Manchurian Incident but I cannot remember exactly when it happened. It did not have a direct impact on my life, so I don't have any particular memories or stories related to it. My life was mostly ordinary during those times.

I did not experience the Great Kantō earthquake personally, but I heard about its aftereffects such as tidal waves and epidemics. It was a major disaster that affected many people, and the stories reached even my area. It was a significant event in Japan's history during my lifetime.

My family's religion was the Shingon Sect of Buddhism. The Shingon Sect is closely related to the Nichiren and Zen sects. Our teacher is Kobo Taishi, also known as Kūkai, and I understand that many believers of this sect live in Shikoku, which is where I was born.

The Nichiren Sect is one of the Buddhist sects related to the Shingon Sect, which was my family's religion. I know it is close to Shingon and Zen Buddhism, but I was raised mainly in the Shingon tradition. Nichiren Buddhism was part of the religious landscape I was aware of growing up in Japan.

Zen Buddhism is another sect related to the Shingon Sect, which was my family's faith. While I was brought up in Shingon Buddhism, I understand that Zen shares some similarities and is also widely practiced in Japan. It was part of the religious environment of my youth.

Kūkai, also known as Kobo Taishi, is the teacher of the Shingon Sect of Buddhism, which was my family's religion. I learned that he is a significant figure especially in Shikoku, where many followers of Shingon Buddhism live. His teachings influenced the religious practices of my family.

I was born in what is now Tosa City in Kochi Prefecture, which is part of Shikoku. Shikoku is known for having many followers of the Shingon Sect of Buddhism, my family's faith. It is a place with strong religious traditions and was my home before I left for the United States in 1906.

When I was young, I heard a little about Christianity, but I never had direct contact with Christians or missionaries in my town. Someone once told me that Christianity would bring happiness to one's bosom when passing away, but it was not part of my family's beliefs. It was something I knew about only in passing.

I stopped over in Hawaii for two days on my way from Japan to the United States mainland in 1906. At that time, many Japanese immigrants went to Hawaii first before continuing on. The Japanese inn there helped us with paperwork, and we got our passports stamped before heading to the mainland. Hawaii was an important transit point for me.

I came to the United States in 1906 to make money and help pay off my father's debts. I worked in various places like Stockton, Sacramento, Riverside, and Los Angeles over the years. Life was hard, but I managed to support myself and later brought my wife here in 1917. The U.S. became my new home for many years.

I was born in Kochi Prefecture, specifically in Takaishimura, Takaoka-gun, now part of Tosa City. My family were part-time farmers and also had a stone-cutting business. It was a rural area with strong ties to Shingon Buddhism, which was our family religion.

I traveled from Japan to Hawaii on a ship called the 'Iminsen,' which was about 12,000 tons. There were three or four ships of this type transporting immigrants like myself. The journey took about twelve days, and most passengers were economy class people seeking work in America.

After arriving in Hawaii, I took a smaller ship called the 'Alameda' to the U.S. West Coast. This part of the journey took about eight days. Most passengers who came from Japan and boarded this ship were people like me, hoping to work and earn money in America.

I first landed in San Francisco before the 1906 earthquake and thought it was a nice town. Unfortunately, anti-Japanese sentiment was strong there, and kids often threw rocks at me, forcing me to run away. I only stayed overnight in San Francisco before moving on to work near Stockton.

I went to an island near Stockton soon after arriving in the U.S. mainland, where I started working in agriculture. A friend gave me the address to find work there, and I stayed in a warehouse at first. Stockton was one of the early places where I worked and lived during my first years in America.

I worked in Sacramento picking crops like grapes and other produce. While picking grapes, I suffered from beriberi and was hospitalized there for about a month. Sacramento was also a place where I spent time recovering and working intermittently during my early years in the U.S.

My wife first came to the United States and worked with me in Riverside after we married in 1917. We spent New Year's Day at a camp in Riverside, and it was one of the places where I worked and lived with my family. Riverside was important in our early married life in America.

After flooding covered the island where I worked, I moved to a Japanese camp in San Leandro. There, I picked crops like green peas and planted tomatoes, even though my legs were still weak from illness. San Leandro was one of the places I worked during my time in California.

I worked as part of a railroad section gang near Naples, California, close to Santa Barbara. The work was not too hard, but the rails rolled dangerously, and after a few days we left because our boss refused to pay us for the initial days. Naples was a brief but memorable work location for me.

Near Santa Barbara, I worked on the railroad in a gang with other Japanese laborers. The weather was nice, but the work was risky due to unstable rails. After a short time, we left the job because the boss would not pay us for the first days we worked.

I moved to Los Angeles after leaving the railroad work near Santa Barbara. There, I worked in the area between Huntington Beach and Santa Ana, mainly cutting celery and later picking oranges and lemons. Los Angeles became a central place for my work and life in southern California.

I worked in the fields between Huntington Beach and Santa Ana for about six months, harvesting celery. There were many Japanese workers in that area, and we had a commission boss who informed us where to work each day. It was part of my agricultural labor experience in California.

Santa Ana was near where I worked harvesting celery alongside Huntington Beach. The work was seasonal, and after celery season ended, I moved on to picking other crops like oranges and lemons. Santa Ana was part of the agricultural region where many Japanese immigrants worked.

I went to Gardena to help a friend during the strawberry season. After the season ended, I was asked to work in the mountain area of Venice, Los Angeles. Gardena was one of the places I worked during my time in California farming various crops.

In Venice, Los Angeles, I worked on a fairly large farm growing barley, potatoes, onions, and corn. The crops were rotated in intervals, and I worked there for about half a year. Venice was an important agricultural work location for me in southern California.
The transcript does not mention Kyutaro Abiko, so I have no personal memories or stories about him.
Nichibei is not mentioned in my story, so I don't have any experiences or knowledge about this organization.
Cortez, California, is not referenced in my experiences or memories, so I cannot provide any information about it.
